criteria: The agent has read Section I completely to understand the motivation for RS3Mamba: CNNs are limited by local receptive fields and Transformers have high computational complexity, while the VSS-based Mamba offers long-range dependency modeling with linear complexity.
The agent has read Section II.A completely to understand the VSS auxiliary encoder and the SS2D mechanism, including the four-direction scanning operations described in Equations 1-3.
The agent has read Fig. 2(a) to understand the detailed VSS block structure: Layer Norm -> Linear -> DWConv -> SS2D -> Layer Norm -> Linear, with a residual connection.

The agent has read Section II.B completely to understand the ResNet18 main encoder and the Collaborative Completion Module (CCM), including how the global branch uses window-based multihead self-attention and the local branch uses convolution.

The agent has read Fig. 2(b) to understand the CCM structure: a global branch applying window-based multihead self-attention to the main branch features, and a local branch applying depthwise convolution to the auxiliary branch features, fused by element-wise addition.

The agent has read Fig. 1 to understand the overall RS3Mamba architecture: four auxiliary encoder stages feed F_a^{1-4} into four CCMs in the main branch, producing F_r^{1-4} as decoder skip connections.

The agent has read Section III.A to understand the ISPRS Vaihingen dataset (16 NIR-R-G orthophotos at ~2500×2000 pixels, 5 foreground classes plus clutter, 12 training / 4 test patches) and the LoveDA Urban dataset (1833 RGB images at 1024×1024, 7 landcover categories, 1156 training / 677 testing).

The agent has read Section III.B to understand the experimental setup: SGD optimizer with lr=0.01, momentum=0.9, weight decay=0.0005, batch size=10, 50 epochs with one test per epoch, on a single NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090.
The agent has read Table I to check the per-class and overall performance results (mF1 and mIoU) of RS3Mamba and all baselines on the ISPRS Vaihingen dataset.
The agent has read Table II to check the per-class and overall performance results of RS3Mamba and all baselines on the LoveDA Urban dataset.
The agent has read Table III to understand the four ablation conditions: Res-b only, VSS-b only, dual-branch with element-wise summation, and full RS3Mamba with CCM.
The agent has read Table IV to check the computational complexity analysis (FLOPs, parameters, memory) for all compared models measured on 256×256 inputs.

The plan includes the overall dual-branch architecture: a VSS auxiliary encoder providing global features, a ResNet18 main encoder providing local features, CCMs at four scales for cross-branch fusion, and a UNetformer decoder.
The plan includes implementing the SS2D module with four-direction scanning using expand (Eq. 1), S6 SSM (Eq. 2), and merge (Eq. 3) operations.
The plan includes the VSS block with Layer Norm, Linear projection, DWConv, and SS2D components as shown in Fig. 2(a).
The plan includes the 4-stage auxiliary encoder: Patch Embedding + VSS block for stage 1, and Patch Merging + VSS block for stages 2-4, producing multi-scale features F_a^{1-4}.
The plan includes the CCM with a global branch (window-based multihead self-attention for F_m^i) and a local branch (convolution for F_a^i), fused by element-wise addition to produce F_r^i.
The plan includes four CCM modules at four corresponding scales (i=1,2,3,4) connecting auxiliary and main encoder features as skip connections to the UNetformer decoder.
The plan includes loading the ISPRS Vaihingen dataset with NIR-R-G three channels, 5 foreground classes, 12 training patches, and 4 test patches.
The plan includes loading the LoveDA Urban dataset with RGB three channels, 7 landcover categories, 1156 training images and 677 test images of size 1024×1024.
The plan includes training with SGD optimizer: learning rate 0.01, momentum 0.9, weight decay 0.0005, batch size 10, for 50 epochs with one test per epoch.
The plan includes implementing all four baselines: ABCNet (ResNet-18), TransUNet (R50-ViT-B), UNetformer (ResNet-18), and CMTFNet (ResNet-50).
The plan includes four ablation variants: (1) Res-b only (UNetformer baseline), (2) VSS-b only, (3) dual-branch with element-wise summation instead of CCM, and (4) full RS3Mamba with CCM.
The plan includes measuring model complexity (FLOPs, parameters, memory) for all compared models using 256×256 input images.
The scan expand operation is implemented as Equation 1: x_v = expand(x, v), creating four separate 1D sequences from the 2D feature map for four scanning directions v∈{1,2,3,4}.

The S6 selective scan SSM is implemented as Equation 2: x̄_v = S6(x_v), processing each directional sequence through the selective state space model that enables interactions between each element and previously scanned samples via a condensed hidden state.
The scan merge operation is implemented as Equation 3: x̄ = merge(x̄_1, x̄_2, x̄_3, x̄_4), combining the four processed directional sequences back into a complete 2D feature map.
The complete SS2D (2D-selective-scan) module is implemented by integrating the expand (Eq. 1), S6 (Eq. 2), and merge (Eq. 3) operations as the core computing unit of the VSS block.
The VSS block includes Layer Normalization applied to the input feature map before the Linear projection and DWConv steps.
The VSS block includes Linear projection layers to expand and project feature dimensions before and after the SS2D module.
The VSS block includes a depthwise convolution (DWConv) applied between the first Linear projection and SS2D to capture local spatial structure.
The complete VSS block is implemented by integrating Layer Norm, Linear, DWConv, SS2D, Layer Norm, and Linear with a residual connection as shown in Fig. 2(a).
The Patch Embedding layer is implemented for stage 1 of the auxiliary encoder, splitting input images X∈R^{H×W×Z} into non-overlapping patches before the first VSS block.
The Patch Merging layers are implemented for stages 2, 3, and 4 of the auxiliary encoder to progressively downsample the feature maps before each subsequent VSS block.
The 4-stage auxiliary encoder is implemented, generating multi-scale features F_a^1 through F_a^4 sequentially, with the entire auxiliary branch executable independently of the main branch.
The ResNet18 main encoder is implemented with 4 residual blocks generating multi-scale feature maps F_m^1 through F_m^4 for local representation learning.
The ResNet18 main encoder is initialized with ImageNet pre-trained weights to leverage existing feature extraction capabilities for remote sensing images.
The CCM global branch is implemented using window-based multihead self-attention (linear complexity, Swin Transformer-style) to model long-range dependencies in the main encoder features F_m^i.
The CCM local branch is implemented using depthwise convolution followed by batch normalization and standard convolution layers to capture local details in the auxiliary encoder features F_a^i.
The CCM combines the global and local branch outputs by element-wise addition to produce the fused cross-branch feature F_r^i.

Four CCM modules are implemented at four corresponding scales (i=1,2,3,4), each taking F_m^i from the main encoder and F_a^i from the auxiliary encoder as inputs and producing F_r^i as skip connections to the decoder.
The UNetformer decoder is implemented to progressively recover spatial resolution using multi-scale skip connections F_r^{1-4} from the CCM and generate the final pixel-level prediction map.
Cross-entropy loss is implemented as the training objective for the segmentation task.
The SGD optimizer is implemented with learning rate 0.01, momentum 0.9, and weight decay (decaying coefficient) 0.0005 for training all models.
The training loop is implemented for 50 total epochs with a batch size of 10 and one evaluation pass per epoch.
The mF1 (mean F1-score) evaluation metric is implemented, computing per-class F1 scores and averaging across foreground classes for quantitative evaluation.
The mIoU (mean intersection over union) evaluation metric is implemented for quantitative performance assessment alongside mF1.

Data loading for ISPRS Vaihingen is implemented: 16 orthophotos in NIR-R-G three channels partitioned into 12 training patches and 4 test patches, covering 5 foreground classes (impervious surface, building, low vegetation, tree, car) and one background class.

Data loading for LoveDA Urban is implemented: 1833 RGB images of size 1024×1024, partitioned into 1156 training and 677 test images across 7 landcover categories (background, building, road, water, barren, forest, agriculture).
Image patch cropping is implemented for training on the Vaihingen dataset, extracting 512×512 sub-patches from the large orthophotos (average size 2500×2000) as shown in Fig. 3.
ABCNet baseline is implemented with ResNet-18 backbone for comparison on both ISPRS Vaihingen and LoveDA Urban datasets.
comment: Section III.B, Table I, Table II

criteria: TransUNet baseline is implemented with R50-ViT-B backbone for comparison on both datasets.
comment: Section III.B, Table I, Table II

criteria: UNetformer baseline is implemented with ResNet-18 backbone as the primary baseline model adopted for RS3Mamba's main encoder and decoder.
comment: Section III.B, Table I, Table II

criteria: CMTFNet baseline is implemented with ResNet-50 backbone for comparison on both datasets.
comment: Section III.B, Table I, Table II

criteria: The ablation variant with only VSS-b (Mamba-based encoder without ResNet main encoder) is implemented to evaluate the standalone performance of VSS-based backbones.
The ablation variant with dual-branch (Res-b + VSS-b) using simple element-wise summation instead of CCM for feature fusion is implemented.
The full RS3Mamba model integrating dual-branch (Res-b + VSS-b) with CCM at all four scales is implemented as the complete proposed architecture.
FLOPs measurement is implemented for complexity analysis of all models using two 256×256 input images on a single NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 GPU.
Model parameter counting is implemented to quantify the scale of each compared model in millions.
GPU memory footprint measurement is implemented to track memory requirements in MB for each model during inference.
RS3Mamba is trained and evaluated on the ISPRS Vaihingen dataset for 50 epochs without errors, producing per-class and overall mF1/mIoU scores.
All four baseline models (ABCNet, TransUNet, UNetformer, CMTFNet) are trained and evaluated on the ISPRS Vaihingen dataset without errors.
RS3Mamba is trained and evaluated on the LoveDA Urban dataset for 50 epochs without errors, producing per-class and overall mF1/mIoU scores.
All four baseline models (ABCNet, TransUNet, UNetformer, CMTFNet) are trained and evaluated on the LoveDA Urban dataset without errors.
All four ablation variants (Res-b only, VSS-b only, dual-branch without CCM, full RS3Mamba) are trained and evaluated on the ISPRS Vaihingen dataset without errors.
Complexity analysis is executed to measure FLOPs, parameters, and memory footprint for all five models (ABCNet, TransUNet, UNetformer, CMTFNet, RS3Mamba) on 256×256 inputs without errors.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table I: RS3Mamba achieves mF1=90.34% and mIoU=82.78% on the ISPRS Vaihingen dataset.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table I: RS3Mamba outperforms all baselines in overall mIoU on Vaihingen (RS3Mamba 82.78% > CMTFNet 82.12% > UNetformer 81.97% > TransUNet 78.16% > ABCNet 75.20%).

The execution outcomes match the per-class F1/IoU results in Table I for RS3Mamba on Vaihingen: impervious surface 92.83/86.62, building 96.82/93.83, low vegetation 80.84/67.84, tree 91.10/83.66, car 90.09/81.97.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table II: RS3Mamba achieves mF1=66.86% and mIoU=50.93% on the LoveDA Urban dataset.

The execution outcomes confirm the results in Table II: RS3Mamba achieves the highest agriculture category performance (50.72%/33.98% F1/IoU), representing the largest improvement over previous methods for the most difficult-to-detect class.
The ablation study results match Table III: the full RS3Mamba (Res-b + VSS-b + CCM) achieves mF1=90.34% and mIoU=82.78%, which is the best among all four ablation configurations.

The ablation results confirm that VSS-b only (mF1=89.04%, mIoU=80.58%) underperforms Res-b only (mF1=89.85%, mIoU=81.97%), demonstrating that current VSS-based backbones without pre-training cannot match pre-trained ResNet18.

The ablation results confirm that dual-branch with element-wise summation (mF1=90.21%, mIoU=82.57%) improves over Res-b alone, and adding CCM further improves to mF1=90.34%/mIoU=82.78%, validating both the dual-branch design and CCM contribution.
The execution outcomes match the complexity analysis in Table IV: RS3Mamba has FLOPs=31.65G, Parameters=43.32M, and Memory=2332MB.

The execution outcomes confirm Table IV's finding that RS3Mamba achieves significantly lower computational complexity than TransUNet (31.65G vs 64.55G FLOPs, 43.32M vs 105.32M parameters) while achieving better mIoU on Vaihingen.
